aliases: Rice with seafood in egg gravy

english: Rice with seafood in egg gravy

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Seafood Mui Fan; mapped to the broader 'rice dish' class (FOODON_03311069) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific entry for Seafood Mui Fan; A00LC (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Seafood Mui Fan is approximately 400 g (one plate), comprising a base of steamed white rice topped with a generous portion of egg-thickened seafood and vegetable gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Seafood Mui Fan — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Seafood Mui Fan (cr_id F05100126). All eight reported nutrients are preserved as-is. Saturated fat and sugar are not reported by HPB for this entry and are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous composite dish data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Seafood Mui Fan. GI estimated at 68 based on the GI of white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted downward to approximately 68 to account for the protein and fat contribution from seafood, egg gravy, and pork, which slow gastric emptying and moderate the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 56.0) / 100 = 38.1, using a 400 g serving with 14.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 208 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 832 mg sodium, representing 36% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce in the gravy and avoid consuming the full gravy portion to keep sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base with 14 g carbohydrate per 100 g, low dietary fibre (0.5 g/100 g), and an estimated GI of 68 (medium-high), producing a high glycaemic load of 38.1 per standard serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a reduced rice portion or substitute brown rice, increase the proportion of vegetables and seafood relative to rice, and avoid the cornstarch-thickened gravy in large quantities to limit postprandial glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 208 mg/100 g and estimated saturated fat at 0.6 g/100 g; oyster sauce and soy sauce contribute to sodium load, while the egg and pork components contribute modest saturated fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake, request minimal gravy, and favour the prawn and squid components over pork to improve the unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very low dietary fibre (0.5 g/100 g) and refined white rice base provide insufficient anti-inflammatory support; processed ingredients in the gravy may carry pro-inflammatory additives.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request fresh prawns and squid only, remove any processed fish cake, and add cruciferous vegetables such as bok choy or broccoli to increase anti-inflammatory phytonutrient content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seafood Mui Fan (海鲜烩饭) is a popular Singaporean Chinese rice dish consisting of a base of steamed white rice topped with a thick, egg-thickened gravy loaded with seafood and vegetables. The standard preparation includes prawns, squid, pork slices, and chye sim (Chinese flowering cabbage), all braised together in a savoury gravy seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil, then thickened with cornstarch and finished with beaten egg to create a silky, glossy sauce. The dish is commonly served as a single-plate meal at hawker centres and coffee shops across Singapore. Texturally, the rice absorbs the umami-rich gravy, while the seafood provides a tender, slightly chewy contrast. Nutritionally, the dish provides 90 kcal per 100 g, with 4.1 g protein, 2.0 g fat, and 14.0 g carbohydrate. Sodium is notable at 208 mg/100 g, making a full 400 g serving contribute approximately 832 mg sodium. The dietary fibre content is low at 0.5 g/100 g. The white rice base carries a medium-to-high glycaemic index (estimated GI 68), producing a high glycaemic load per serving, which is clinically relevant for patients managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The dish is not suitable for halal diets due to the pork component. It is dairy-free and provides modest calcium (21 mg/100 g) and iron (0.8 mg/100 g) from the seafood and vegetable components.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F05100126), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat and sugar are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous data and clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database basis, documented transparently.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Seafood Mui Fan (F05100126) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite dish as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at 0.6 g/100 g and sugar estimated at 0.5 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central analogous mixed rice dish with seafood and egg gravy; these are approximations and should be replaced with direct measurement data when available. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI (~72, University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted for protein and fat moderating effect from seafood and egg components. The input health_suitability field referenced 'fish cake' as an ingredient; the canonical HPB description lists prawn, pork, chye sim, and squid — fish cake is not confirmed in the canonical record and has not been incorporated into the classification. Halal status is false due to the presence of pork.
